ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn entry for Chicken Salan; closest match is chicken curry (FOODON_03315595). Chicken Salan is a Hyderabadi nut-and-seed-based gravy dish distinct from generic chicken curry.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Chicken Salan. A0CKN (cooked chicken meat) used as the closest defensible anchor. The dish includes a peanut-sesame-tamarind gravy not captured by this code.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Chicken Salan or a sufficiently close analogue in the clinical food hierarchy.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard side-dish serving of Chicken Salan is approximately 250 g, typically ladled alongside Hyderabadi Biryani or Bagara Khana.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Chicken Salan as a composite dish. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked chicken, peanuts, sesame seeds, coconut, and tamarind in proportions consistent with a standard Hyderabadi Salan recipe. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy used as a secondary cross-check for micronutrients. Values represent a reasonable estimate for a home-style preparation.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — peer-reviewed analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Chicken Salan. GI estimated at 30 (low) based on analogous mixed chicken-and-legume/nut curry entries in the University of Sydney GI Database. The high protein content of chicken, healthy fats from peanuts and sesame seeds, and acidity from tamarind all attenuate postprandial glucose response, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 12.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 9.4 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content 300 mg per 100 g; a 250 g serving contributes 750 mg sodium (~33% of the 2300 mg daily limit).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced added salt during preparation, while benefiting from the potassium (225 mg/100 g) and unsaturated fats from peanuts and sesame that support vascular health.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~30) driven by high protein, healthy fats, dietary fibre, and tamarind acidity, resulting in attenuated postprandial glucose response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chicken Salan is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as a side dish (250 g) alongside a controlled portion of low-GI rice or roti, as the low GI and moderate protein content support glycaemic control; total carbohydrate intake from the accompanying staple should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 4.5 g per 100 g and sodium 300 mg per 100 g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; peanuts and sesame provide cardioprotective unsaturated fats and phytosterols.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume Chicken Salan in moderate portions (150–200 g) prepared with minimal added oil, as the unsaturated fats from peanuts and sesame seeds offer cardiovascular benefit, but the saturated fat and sodium content warrant portion control and reduced-salt preparation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory compounds from ginger, turmeric, and sesame seeds; potential allergenicity of peanuts and sesame in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Chicken Salan may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory properties of turmeric and ginger, but those with peanut or sesame allergies or sensitivities should avoid this dish.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Salan is a sophisticated Hyderabadi side dish from South India, closely related to the celebrated Mirchi Ka Salan. It features bone-in or boneless chicken pieces slow-simmered in a distinctive 'Salan' gravy built on a roasted and ground paste of peanuts, sesame seeds, and dry coconut, enriched with whole spices including cumin, coriander, and dried red chillies, and finished with a sharp tamarind reduction. The dish has a relatively thin, soupy consistency — unlike cream-based North Indian curries — with a complex, multi-layered flavour profile combining nuttiness, earthy warmth, and a bright tangy finish. Texturally, the chicken is tender and the gravy coats lightly. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 187.5 kcal, 8.75 g protein, 11.25 g fat (predominantly unsaturated from peanuts and sesame), and 12.5 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 30 (low), making it suitable for blood sugar management. Sodium is moderate at 300 mg/100 g, warranting portion awareness for hypertensive individuals. It is a good source of niacin (3.8 mg), phosphorus (145 mg), magnesium (32 mg), and vitamin B12 (0.4 mcg). Chicken Salan is traditionally served as an accompaniment to Hyderabadi Biryani or Bagara Khana (tempered rice) and is a staple of festive and Sunday family meals across Hyderabad and the broader Deccan region. It is naturally gluten-free and dairy-free, and is halal-compliant when prepared with halal-certified chicken.
common_user_queries: How is Chicken Salan different from regular chicken curry?
Is Chicken Salan good for diabetics?
What are the calories in Chicken Salan?
What do you eat Chicken Salan with?
What nuts are in Chicken Salan?

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central entry exists for Chicken Salan as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN component data for cooked chicken, peanuts, sesame seeds, coconut, and tamarind. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium rather than high because the values represent a modelled estimate rather than a direct laboratory measurement of this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite Hyderabadi dish are not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as a single entry; component-level data exist but cannot be reliably aggregated without recipe-specific oil and nut proportions. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in available approved sources. trans_fat_g value of 0.13 g/100 g is retained from the input record; this is plausible given the use of cooking oil in preparation. Portions in the input record contained four duplicate entries for '1 serving (250g)'; these have been deduplicated to a single 250 g serving as the default. The '1 plate (400g)' entry from the input (sourced from a delivery platform) has been excluded as delivery platforms are a forbidden source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (30) × carbohydrate_g per serving (12.5 × 2.5 = 31.25 g) / 100 = 9.4 g per 250 g serving. Allergen note: peanuts and sesame are major allergens; individuals with tree-nut or groundnut allergies should avoid this dish.
